events.text: The patient is a 62-year-old female named Margaret Chen. She is a former smoker with a 25 pack-year history, having quit 8 years prior to diagnosis. She has a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. She works as a retired accountant and lives with her husband.

At age 62 years, the patient received a diagnosis of Stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0, AJCC 8th edition) NON-SMALL CELL LUNG CARCINOMA, ADENOCARCINOMA (ICD-O-3 histology code 8140/3), primary site: UPPER LOBE OF LEFT LUNG (ICD-O-3 site code C34.1). Summary stage: Regional. Key biomarkers: EGFR L858R mutation detected (exon 21 point mutation); PD-L1 TPS 15%; ALK IHC negative; ROS1 FISH negative; KRAS wild-type. Tumor size: 4.2 cm primary mass with ipsilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via CT-guided biopsy. Laterality: Left. Tumor grade: Poorly differentiated (Grade 3). Performance status: ECOG 1.

At age 62 years, the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (staging), which showed a 4.2 cm spiculated left upper lobe mass, mediastinal lymphadenopathy (stations 4L, 5, 6), left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy, and no evidence of distant metastatic disease. No pleural effusion. No liver, adrenal, or bone lesions identified. Cancer present.

At age 62 years, the patient had a PET-CT WHOLE BODY (staging), which showed FDG-avid left upper lobe primary mass (SUVmax 12.4), FDG-avid mediastinal and left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (SUVmax up to 8.7), no evidence of distant organ or bone metastases. Findings consistent with stage IIIB disease. Cancer present.

At age 62 years, the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM (staging), which showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Normal study. Cancer absent.

At age 62 years, the patient had a pathology result from CT-guided left upper lobe core needle biopsy. Diagnosis: Invasive adenocarcinoma, acinar predominant pattern (60%), with solid components (40%). Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A positive, CK7 positive, CK20 negative, p40 negative. Consistent with primary lung adenocarcinoma. Molecular testing ordered. Specimen adequacy: Adequate for molecular profiling.

At age 62 years, the patient had next generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, based on a specimen obtained from the left upper lobe primary tumor (CT-guided biopsy), which showed an EGFR p.L858R (exon 21) activating mutation (allele frequency 38%), a TP53 p.R175H missense mutation (allele frequency 42%), a CDKN2A homozygous deletion (copy number loss), and an NKX2-1 (TTF-1) amplification. No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, MET, RET, ROS1, ALK, ERBB2, or FGFR alterations detected. TMB: 4 mutations/Mb (low). MSI: Stable. No T790M resistance mutation detected at baseline.

At age 62 years, the patient had a thoracic oncology multidisciplinary tumor board discussion. Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) left lung adenocarcinoma with EGFR L858R mutation. Given N3 disease (contralateral supraclavicular involvement), concurrent chemoradiation was considered but consensus favored systemic therapy with osimertinib given the sensitizing EGFR mutation, as per emerging data for EGFR-mutant stage III disease. Decision: Initiate osimertinib 80 mg daily as first-line systemic therapy. Radiation oncology to evaluate for potential consolidation thoracic radiotherapy. Cancer present. No response assessment yet as treatment not started.

At age 62 years, the patient initiated first-line systemic therapy with osimertinib (TAGRISSO) 80 mg orally once daily for stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma. Treatment intent: Curative-intent (definitive systemic therapy). Cycle 1 Day 1.

At age 62 years (approximately 4 weeks after starting osimertinib), the patient developed grade 2 acneiform rash over the face and upper chest, and grade 1 diarrhea (2-3 loose stools/day). Managed with topical clindamycin and doxycycline 100 mg daily for rash; loperamide PRN for diarrhea. No dose reduction required. These events were attributed to osimertinib (CTCAE v5.0: Rash acneiform Grade 2, Diarrhea Grade 1).

At age 62 years (3 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (response assessment), which showed significant reduction of the left upper lobe primary mass from 4.2 cm to 2.1 cm (50% reduction by RECIST 1.1), decreased mediastinal lymphadenopathy, near-resolution of supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Assessment: Partial response. No new lesions. Cancer present but responding.

At age 62 years (3 months on osimertinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. CT showing excellent partial response (50% reduction in primary tumor). Patient tolerating therapy well with manageable grade 2 rash, now improving. QTc 432 ms on ECG (within acceptable range). Continuing osimertinib 80 mg daily. Cancer present with response to therapy.

At age 63 years (9 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (response assessment), which showed continued partial response with left upper lobe mass now measuring 1.5 cm (64% reduction from baseline), minimal residual mediastinal adenopathy. No evidence of new metastatic disease in chest, abdomen, or pelvis. Assessment: Ongoing partial response by RECIST 1.1. Cancer present but responding.

At age 63 years (9 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM (surveillance), which showed no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No new lesions. Cancer absent.

At age 63 years (9 months on osimertinib), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Continued partial response on imaging. Patient remains ECOG 1 with well-controlled rash (now grade 1). Paronychia grade 1 of left index finger noted, managed with warm soaks and topical antibiotics. No evidence of brain metastases on surveillance MRI. Continuing osimertinib 80 mg daily. Cancer present with ongoing response.

At age 63 years (14 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (response assessment), which showed interval increase in left upper lobe residual mass from 1.5 cm to 2.8 cm, new left hilar lymphadenopathy, new small right adrenal nodule measuring 1.1 cm (new from prior). Assessment: Progressive disease by RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, progression documented.

At age 63 years (14 months after initiating osimertinib), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM (prompted by progression on CT), which showed three new enhancing brain metastases: right frontal (1.4 cm), right parietal (0.8 cm), and left cerebellar hemisphere (0.6 cm). No leptomeningeal enhancement. Mass effect minimal. Cancer present, new brain metastases.

At age 63 years (14 months on osimertinib, at progression), the patient had an urgent oncologist office assessment. CT demonstrating systemic progression, MRI showing three new brain metastases. Patient currently neurologically intact (ECOG 1), no headaches, no focal deficits. Decision to obtain liquid biopsy (cell-free DNA) for resistance mechanism profiling and to re-biopsy adrenal nodule. Urgently referred to radiation oncology for stereotactic radiosurgery evaluation for brain metastases. Osimertinib held pending further workup. Cancer present, actively progressing.

At age 63 years (at time of progression), the patient had a pathology result from CT-guided right adrenal core needle biopsy. Diagnosis: Metastatic adenocarcinoma, consistent with lung primary origin. Immunohistochemistry: TTF-1 positive, Napsin-A focally positive, CK7 positive. Morphology consistent with metastatic lung adenocarcinoma. Molecular testing (NGS and EGFR T790M) ordered from this specimen.

At age 63 years (progression biopsy), the patient had next generation sequencing performed for non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, based on a specimen obtained from the right adrenal metastasis (CT-guided biopsy), which showed an EGFR p.L858R (exon 21) activating mutation (allele frequency 44%), an EGFR p.T790M (exon 20) resistance mutation (allele frequency 31%), a TP53 p.R175H missense mutation (allele frequency 47%), a CDKN2A homozygous deletion, an NKX2-1 amplification, and a PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (allele frequency 19%, newly acquired). No MET amplification. No EGFR C797S detected. TMB: 5 mutations/Mb (low). MSI: Stable. The T790M and PIK3CA mutations are consistent with acquired resistance to first-generation/third-generation EGFR TKI therapy.

At age 63 years (14 months after starting osimertinib, approximately 3 weeks after progression documented), the patient received stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to three brain metastases: right frontal (1.4 cm, 20 Gy in 1 fraction), right parietal (0.8 cm, 20 Gy in 1 fraction), and left cerebellar hemisphere (0.6 cm, 18 Gy in 1 fraction). Treatment delivered using Gamma Knife Perfexion platform. Treatment intent: Palliative/local control. Total brain irradiation deferred given oligometastatic burden and desire to preserve neurocognitive function.

At age 63 years (2 weeks post-SRS), the patient developed grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 headache following stereotactic radiosurgery. Treated with dexamethasone 4 mg twice daily for 5 days with taper. No seizures. No neurological deficits. Symptoms resolved within 2 weeks of completing the steroid taper. Attributed to SRS-related cerebral edema (CTCAE v5.0: Fatigue Grade 2, Headache Grade 1).

At age 63 years (approximately 6 weeks after progression documentation, following recovery from SRS), the patient initiated second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin (AUC 5 IV day 1) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2 IV day 1) every 21 days for stage IV (M1c) EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma with acquired T790M resistance mutation and brain metastases. Treatment intent: Palliative. Osimertinib was discontinued. Folic acid 400 mcg daily and vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks initiated per pemetrexed protocol. Dexamethasone premedication given.

At age 63 years (during carboplatin/pemetrexed chemotherapy, cycle 2), the patient developed grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.7 x 10^9/L), grade 2 nausea, and grade 1 fatigue. Carboplatin/pemetrexed dose delayed by 1 week; filgrastim 480 mcg subcutaneously daily for 5 days administered. Ondansetron and prochlorperazine prescribed for nausea. Counts recovered; cycle 3 administered at full dose with G-CSF prophylaxis thereafter (CTCAE v5.0: Neutrophil count decreased Grade 3, Nausea Grade 2, Fatigue Grade 1).

At age 63 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (response assessment), which showed stable left upper lobe mass (2.8 cm, unchanged), stable right adrenal nodule (1.1 cm, unchanged), no new systemic metastases. Assessment: Stable disease by RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, neither responding nor progressing.

At age 63 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM (follow-up post-SRS), which showed stable treatment effect at all three prior SRS sites (right frontal, right parietal, left cerebellar), with no new brain metastases. Mild radiation-related change at right frontal SRS site (expected). Cancer absent (no active intracranial disease).

At age 63 years (after 3 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. Imaging showing stable systemic disease and good intracranial control post-SRS. Patient tolerating chemotherapy with managed toxicities (grade 3 neutropenia in cycle 2, now on G-CSF prophylaxis). Completed 4 cycles total of carboplatin/pemetrexed, transitioning to pemetrexed maintenance as per standard of care. ECOG PS 1. Discussed clinical trial options given T790M/PIK3CA co-resistance profile. Cancer present, disease stable.

At age 64 years (after completing 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed induction), the patient initiated pemetrexed maintenance therapy (500 mg/m2 IV every 21 days) for stage IV EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma following carboplatin/pemetrexed induction. Continued folic acid and vitamin B12 supplementation. Treatment intent: Palliative/maintenance.

At age 64 years (after 3 cycles of pemetrexed maintenance, approximately 9 months after SRS and chemotherapy initiation), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (response assessment), which showed interval progression: left upper lobe mass increased from 2.8 cm to 4.0 cm, right adrenal nodule increased from 1.1 cm to 2.0 cm, new small hepatic lesion in segment VI measuring 0.9 cm (new from prior), new right-sided pleural effusion (small). Assessment: Progressive disease by RECIST 1.1. Cancer present, actively progressing.

At age 64 years (at second progression), the patient had an oncologist office assessment. CT demonstrating systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance with new liver metastasis and increasing pleural effusion. ECOG PS 1-2. MRI brain ordered. Discussed transition to clinical trial. Patient enrolled in Phase I/II trial evaluating a novel EGFR/PIK3CA dual inhibitor (protocol NCT-XXXXXX) given co-existence of acquired T790M and PIK3CA E545K resistance mutations. Pemetrexed discontinued. Baseline assessments for trial initiated. Cancer present, progressing.

At age 64 years (at second progression, pre-clinical trial enrollment), the patient had an MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM, which showed stable post-treatment changes at prior SRS sites with no new intracranial metastases. One prior SRS lesion (right frontal) showed central necrosis consistent with radiation necrosis vs. pseudoprogression, stable in size at 1.4 cm. No new enhancing lesions. Cancer absent (stable intracranial disease, no new metastases).

At age 64 years, the patient enrolled in and initiated treatment on a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational EGFR exon 20/C797S/T790M-resistant inhibitor combined with a PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor (alpelisib analog) for patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC with acquired PIK3CA co-mutations. Trial arm: Experimental arm (investigational EGFR inhibitor + PI3K inhibitor combination). Treatment intent: Palliative/investigational. Baseline labs, ECG, ophthalmologic exam, and fasting glucose completed per trial protocol. Cycle 1 Day 1.

At age 64 years (cycle 2 of clinical trial therapy), the patient developed grade 2 hyperglycemia (fasting glucose 210 mg/dL) attributed to PI3K inhibitor component, grade 1 mucositis, and grade 1 rash. Metformin dose increased; dietary counseling provided. No dose reduction required at this time. Glucose monitored weekly. CTCAE v5.0: Hyperglycemia Grade 2, Mucositis oral Grade 1, Rash maculopapular Grade 1.

At age 64 years (after 2 cycles of clinical trial therapy, approximately 8 weeks on trial), the patient had a CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST (trial response assessment, RECIST 1.1), which showed decrease in left upper lobe mass from 4.0 cm to 3.0 cm, stable right adrenal nodule (2.0 cm), decrease in hepatic segment VI lesion from 0.9 cm to 0.6 cm, near-resolution of pleural effusion. Assessment: Partial response by RECIST 1.1 (25% reduction in sum of target lesions). Cancer present but responding to investigational therapy.

At age 64 years (on clinical trial, after 2 cycles), the patient had an oncologist/trial site office assessment. CT showing early partial response on investigational therapy. Patient tolerating trial therapy with manageable hyperglycemia (now grade 1 with metformin adjustment), mild mucositis and rash. ECOG PS 1. QTc 438 ms on serial ECG monitoring. Patient motivated and engaged with trial participation. Will continue on protocol with next response assessment at cycle 4 (8-week mark). Cancer present with response to investigational therapy.

documents.text:
CT CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

CLINICAL INDICATION: 62-year-old female with known stage IIIB left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant. Currently on osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily for approximately 3 months. Imaging requested for treatment response assessment.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast obtained at time of initial staging.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast. Images were acquired during portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis and during peak arterial enhancement for the chest. Slice thickness 1.25 mm reconstructed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. No oral contrast was administered. Images reviewed on PACS workstation with multiplanar reformats.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways: There has been marked interval decrease in the size of the left upper lobe primary mass. The spiculated, pleural-based mass now measures approximately 2.1 cm in its greatest axial dimension, compared to 4.2 cm on the prior staging examination, representing a roughly 50% decrease in size. The lesion retains a degree of irregular speculation along its margins, though the overall conspicuity and soft tissue density are substantially reduced compared to the prior study. There is no surrounding ground-glass opacity, consolidation, or satellite nodularity to suggest local spread. The remaining visible lung parenchyma demonstrates no new focal consolidation, mass, or nodular lesion suspicious for intrapulmonary metastatic disease. The central airways are patent bilaterally. No endobronchial lesion is identified. No obstructive post-obstructive atelectasis or pneumonia is seen. Mild background centrilobular emphysematous changes are present bilaterally, consistent with the patient's prior smoking history.

Mediastinum and Lymph Nodes: The previously prominent mediastinal lymph nodes have decreased substantially in size. A cluster of left paratracheal and aortopulmonary window nodes (stations 4L, 5, and 6), which had been enlarged and confluent on the prior scan, now appear significantly smaller. The largest residual node in the aortopulmonary window measures approximately 0.9 cm in short axis, compared to prior measurements in the 1.5 to 2.0 cm range. These nodes retain mildly indistinct margins but are no longer enlarged by size criteria. No new mediastinal adenopathy is identified.

Left Supraclavicular Region: There has been near-complete resolution of the left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy that was conspicuous on the prior staging study. The soft tissue fullness previously noted in this region has largely resolved, with no discrete nodal mass remaining identifiable on this examination. This represents a favorable response in the region corresponding to the N3 nodal disease that established the stage IIIB designation.

Heart and Pericardium: The cardiac silhouette is normal in size. No pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta and visible great vessels are unremarkable. No mediastinal widening.

Pleural Spaces: No pleural effusion identified bilaterally. No pneumothorax. No pleural thickening or nodularity.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax: No suspicious osseous lesions are identified. The visualized ribs, thoracic vertebral bodies, and scapulae demonstrate no lytic or sclerotic lesions concerning for metastatic disease. No pathologic fracture. Mild degenerative changes are noted in the thoracic spine.

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size with a smooth hepatic contour. No focal hepatic lesion is identified. No intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation. No periportal edema.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: The gallbladder is present and unremarkable. The common bile duct is not dilated.

Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates normal attenuation and contour throughout the head, body, and tail. No pancreatic mass, ductal dilatation, or peripancreatic inflammatory change.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No splenic lesion.

Adrenal Glands: Both adrenal glands appear within normal limits. The right adrenal gland demonstrates normal limb thickness and morphology with no nodular enlargement. The left adrenal gland is similarly unremarkable. No adrenal mass is identified on this study.

Kidneys and Ureters: Both kidneys enhance symmetrically and demonstrate normal corticomedullary differentiation. A few bilateral simple-appearing cortical cysts are present, the largest measuring approximately 1.2 cm in the right kidney, unchanged in appearance and benign in nature. No solid renal mass. No hydronephrosis. No urolithiasis identified.

Gastrointestinal Tract: The stomach, small bowel, and visualized large bowel loops are unremarkable in caliber and wall thickness. No bowel obstruction. No free intraperitoneal air. No peritoneal implants or ascites.

Retroperitoneum and Lymph Nodes: No retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No para-aortic nodal enlargement. The aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber.

PELVIS:

Bladder: The urinary bladder is partially distended and demonstrates uniform wall thickness without focal lesion.

Gynecologic Structures: The uterus is not well visualized on this examination, consistent with the patient's post-menopausal status and likely prior involution. No adnexal mass or pelvic free fluid is identified.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes: No enlarged pelvic or inguinal lymph nodes are identified.

Bony Pelvis: The visualized pelvic bones, including the iliac wings, sacrum, and proximal femora, demonstrate no suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions. No cortical disruption or soft tissue mass associated with the osseous structures.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Marked interval decrease in size of the left upper lobe primary lung mass, now measuring approximately 2.1 cm compared to 4.2 cm on the prior staging examination. This represents a substantial and favorable response to osi therapy.

2. Significantly decreased mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 4L, 5, and 6, with residual nodes now at or below the threshold for pathologic enlargement by size criteria.

3. Near-complete resolution of left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy previously documented as the basis for N3 nodal staging.

4. No pleural effusion. No new pulmonary nodules or intrapulmonary lesions suspicious for metastatic spread.

5. No hepatic, adrenal, or other abdominal/pelvic lesions to suggest distant metastatic disease.

6. No suspicious osseous lesions in the visualized axial and appendicular skeleton.

Overall, the findings are highly consistent with a meaningful and ongoing therapeutic response to EGFR-directed therapy. Clinical and imaging correlation with the treatment team is recommended for further management planning.

THORACIC ONCOLOGY CLINIC - FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Osimertinib (TAGRISSO) Therapy -- 9-Month Response Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Ms. Margaret Chen is a 63-year-old woman with stage IIIB (T3N3M0) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, presenting for her 9-month response assessment visit while on osimertinib 80 mg daily. She presents for routine assessment, restaging imaging review, and management of ongoing treatment-related toxicities.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen is a 63-year-old former smoker (25 pack-year history, quit approximately 8-9 years ago) with a history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus who was diagnosed approximately 9 months ago with stage IIIB non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma (acinar-predominant, Grade 3/poorly differentiated), arising from the left upper lobe. At the time of diagnosis, the primary mass measured 4.2 cm with mediastinal lymphadenopathy involving stations 4L, 5, and 6, and ipsilateral supraclavicular lymph node involvement, confirmed on both CT and PET-CT staging. Brain MRI at diagnosis was negative for intracranial disease. CT-guided biopsy confirmed adenocarcinoma; next-generation sequencing revealed EGFR L858R exon 21 activating mutation (allele frequency 38%), TP53 R175H co-mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification. No ALK, ROS1, MET, or ERBB2 alterations. TMB was low at 4 mutations/Mb.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion, given her N3 disease (supraclavicular involvement) and sensitizing EGFR mutation, the consensus was to pursue upfront systemic therapy with osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily rather than concurrent chemoradiation. Thoracic radiation oncology was consulted at that time and deferred consolidative radiotherapy pending systemic response.

She initiated osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily approximately 9 months ago. Her 3-month response assessment CT demonstrated significant tumor shrinkage -- the primary mass reduced from 4.2 cm to 2.1 cm (roughly 50% reduction), with decreased mediastinal lymphadenopathy and near-resolution of supraclavicular adenopathy, consistent with a meaningful partial response. At that visit, she was also noted to have grade 2 acneiform rash (on the face and upper chest) managed with topical clindamycin and oral doxycycline, which was improving, and grade 1 diarrhea managed with loperamide PRN.

She now presents today following her 9-month restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain (both reviewed in detail below). Since the last visit approximately 6 months ago, she reports continued good overall tolerance of the medication. The acneiform rash has substantially improved and she characterizes it now as mild -- intermittent small pustules on the nasal bridge and chin, no longer requiring oral doxycycline. She completed approximately 3 months of doxycycline and discontinued it without recurrence of severe rash. She is still applying topical clindamycin lotion to affected areas as needed. Diarrhea has largely resolved; she occasionally takes loperamide once daily but has not required it in the past 2-3 weeks.

Of note, she has developed tenderness and mild swelling of the lateral nail fold of the left index finger over the past 4-6 weeks, which she attributes to the Tagrisso. She has been doing warm soaks at home twice daily and applied over-the-counter antibiotic ointment, with modest improvement but not complete resolution. No frank purulent discharge, no deep tissue infection, no involvement of other digits at this time.

She denies any new respiratory symptoms -- no increased dyspnea, no hemoptysis, no new chest pain. She notes mild and intermittent nonproductive cough, which she believes is unchanged or slightly improved compared to before treatment. No wheezing or stridor. Appetite is good. She has maintained her weight and notes she has actually gained approximately 2 lbs since last visit. No nausea or vomiting. No significant fatigue limiting her daily activities. She is still living at home with her husband, remains relatively active, walks 20-30 minutes daily, and manages household activities independently. She has been retired since prior to diagnosis.

Cardiovascular review: No palpitations, no presyncope, no lower extremity edema. She continues on her antihypertensive regimen (amlodipine and lisinopril) with blood pressures reportedly well controlled at home. Diabetes is managed with metformin; she monitors fasting glucose at home and reports values consistently in the 110-130 mg/dL range.

She denies any headaches, visual changes, focal weakness, sensory changes, or cognitive difficulties that might suggest intracranial disease.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
- Constitutional: No fever, no night sweats. Weight stable (gained ~2 lbs). No significant fatigue.
- Respiratory: Mild intermittent dry cough (unchanged to improved). No dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea on vigorous activity (baseline, unchanged from prior). No hemoptysis. No pleuritic pain.
- Cardiovascular: No palpitations, no chest pain, no lower extremity edema.
- Gastrointestinal: Occasional soft stool, no active diarrhea. Good appetite. No nausea. No abdominal pain.
- Skin/integumentary: Grade 1 acneiform rash (nasal bridge and chin, improved from prior grade 2). Grade 1 paronychia left index finger (new since last visit). No rash over trunk.
- Neurological: No headache. No focal neurological deficits. No cognitive changes.
- Musculoskeletal: No new bone pain or arthralgias.
- Endocrine/metabolic: Fasting glucose well-controlled per home monitoring. No polyuria or polydipsia.
- Ophthalmologic: No visual disturbances.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 128/76 mmHg
- Heart rate: 72 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 16 breaths/min
- SpO2: 97% on room air
- Temperature: 37.0 degrees C
- Weight: 62.3 kg (up from 61.8 kg at prior visit)
- ECOG Performance Status: 1

General: Alert and oriented, in no acute distress. Well-nourished, well-appearing woman who looks her stated age. Engaged and conversational.

Head and Neck:
- Skin: Small scattered pustular lesions on the nasal bridge and chin consistent with mild (grade 1) acneiform rash. No involvement of the forehead or scalp compared to prior. No lichenification. No erosions.
- HEENT: Atraumatic, normocephalic. Mucous membranes moist. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.
- Neck: Supple. No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Left supraclavicular region that was previously palpably enlarged is now flat and non-palpable -- a notable improvement from the palpable adenopathy present at diagnosis.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Breath sounds present bilaterally. No wheezing. Faint decreased breath sounds over the left upper lobe region compared to the right -- consistent with known tumor and post-treatment changes. No crackles. No egophony. No pleural friction rub.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normoactive bowel sounds. No hepatomegaly. No splenomegaly. No palpable masses.

Extremities:
- Left index finger: Grade 1 paronychia with mild periungual erythema, mild soft tissue swelling of the lateral nail fold. No fluctuance. No pus. No signs of proximal spread or involvement of deep structures. Nail plate intact. Remaining digits unremarkable.
- No clubbing. No cyanosis.

Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait normal. No cerebellar signs.

Lymph Nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. As noted, previously palpable left supraclavicular node is no longer palpable.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today)

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.8 x 10^9/L (reference 4.0-11.0)
- Hemoglobin: 12.4 g/dL (reference 12.0-16.0)
- Platelets: 198 x 10^9/L (reference 150-400)
- Differential: unremarkable, no neutropenia

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.0 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 0.82 mg/dL (baseline for patient)
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- ALT: 28 U/L (within normal limits)
- AST: 22 U/L (within normal limits)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L
- Albumin: 4.0 g/dL

Glucose (fasting): 124 mg/dL (mildly elevated, stable for her)
HbA1c: 6.8% (stable glycemic control)

Magnesium: 0.82 mmol/L (within normal limits)

Electrocardiogram (performed today):
- Sinus rhythm at 72 bpm
- Normal axis
- QTc: 432 ms (Bazett correction) -- within acceptable range; no prolongation. Baseline QTc prior to osimertinib was approximately 418 ms. Interval has been stable across prior assessments. No delta waves, no bundle branch block.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with Contrast (9-month restaging):
Reviewed in detail. The left upper lobe primary mass has continued to shrink, now measuring approximately 1.5 cm in maximum diameter compared to 2.1 cm at the 3-month scan and 4.2 cm at baseline -- representing an approximately 64% reduction from the original tumor size. The margins appear less spiculated compared to the baseline scan, with a more rounded configuration. There is residual perihilar soft tissue thickening, but the previously bulky mediastinal adenopathy has markedly decreased, with only minimal residual soft tissue in the subcarinal region and the aortopulmonary window that is no longer convincingly pathologic. No new pulmonary nodules. No evidence of pleural effusion. No new hilar adenopathy. Hepatic parenchyma appears homogeneous with no focal lesions. Adrenal glands appear normal bilaterally. No retroperitoneal adenopathy. No osseous lesions. Overall, the study is highly consistent with ongoing, deepening partial response with continued shrinkage of the primary tumor and nearly complete resolution of mediastinal adenopathy.

MRI Brain with and without Gadolinium (9-month surveillance):
No enhancing parenchymal lesions identified. No new intracranial metastases. No leptomeningeal enhancement. No hydrocephalus. No midline shift. The examination is reassuring with no evidence of intracranial disease, consistent with her prior brain MRI obtained at diagnosis.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, on osimertinib 80 mg daily -- ONGOING PARTIAL RESPONSE.

Ms. Chen continues to demonstrate excellent and deepening response to osimertinib. The primary tumor has now shrunk by approximately 64% from baseline (from 4.2 cm to 1.5 cm), mediastinal adenopathy is nearly resolved, the previously palpable left supraclavicular node is no longer evident on clinical exam, and brain MRI confirms no intracranial disease. This is a gratifying and sustained response. The progression-free duration on osimertinib in EGFR-mutant NSCLC can vary but median PFS in stage IV EGFR-mutant disease approximates 18 months; in this stage III setting, durability of response remains an important goal.

Plan:
- Continue osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily. No dose modification required.
- Schedule next response assessment CT chest/abdomen/pelvis in approximately 3 months.
- Continue surveillance MRI brain every 6 months given EGFR-mutant NSCLC and the known CNS penetration advantages of osimertinib over earlier-generation TKIs.
- Continue to defer radiation oncology consolidation discussion given ongoing excellent systemic response; will revisit if/when disease stabilizes further or at the next major imaging interval.

2. EGFR TKI-related acneiform rash -- Grade 1, improved.

Rash has improved from grade 2 to grade 1 over the past 6 months with discontinuation of oral doxycycline and ongoing use of topical clindamycin. Current involvement is limited to the nasal bridge and chin without significant functional impairment or discomfort. No dose modification indicated at this time.

Plan:
- Continue topical clindamycin lotion applied to affected areas once to twice daily as needed.
- Patient counseled to use gentle, fragrance-free moisturizer and high-SPF sunscreen when outdoors.
- Oral doxycycline may be reinitiated if rash worsens to grade 2 again.
- No dose reduction necessary at current grade 1 severity.

3. EGFR TKI-related paronychia -- Grade 1, left index finger.

New since the last visit. Paronychia is a well-recognized class effect of EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors, occurring in roughly 10-15% of patients. This is presenting as grade 1 periungual inflammation without infection or significant functional compromise.

Plan:
- Warm soaks 10-15 minutes twice daily to the affected digit.
- Apply topical high-potency antibiotic (mupirocin 2% ointment) to the periungual area twice daily -- prescribed today.
- Patients should be instructed to wear well-fitting footwear and avoid tight gloves; advised to trim nails straight across and avoid lateral nail trimming that may worsen lateral fold irritation.
- If paronychia worsens to grade 2 or develops purulent superinfection, will consider oral antibiotics (tetracyclines preferred given concurrent EGFR inhibitor therapy) and possible dermatology referral.
- No dose modification at this time.

4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- stable.

Fasting glucose today 124 mg/dL; HbA1c 6.8%, indicating adequate glycemic control on metformin. Blood pressure well-controlled on amlodipine and lisinopril.

Plan:
- Continue metformin per primary care provider. No changes indicated at this visit.
- Reinforce dietary counseling given ongoing anti-cancer therapy.
- Continue antihypertensives as prescribed.

5. QTc monitoring.

Osimertinib carries a risk of QTc prolongation. QTc today is 432 ms, stable compared to prior assessment (432 ms at 3 months) and slightly above patient's personal baseline (approximately 418 ms pre-treatment), but well within acceptable range (<470 ms for women per institutional protocol). No action required beyond ongoing monitoring.

Plan:
- Continue routine ECG at each follow-up visit while on osimertinib.
- Electrolytes within normal range today; encourage oral hydration and dietary magnesium.
- Will hold osimertinib if QTc exceeds 500 ms or increases >60 ms from baseline.

6. Return to clinic.

Ms. Chen will return to clinic in approximately 3 months for the next response assessment visit, coinciding with her 12-month mark on osimertinib. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be ordered in advance of that visit. She has clinic nursing contact information and is instructed to call with any new or worsening symptoms -- particularly dyspnea, new neurological symptoms, significant rash flare, chest pain, or palpitations -- in advance of her scheduled visit.

Time spent: approximately 45 minutes, majority in counseling and care coordination.

MRI BRAIN WITH AND WITHOUT GADOLINIUM CONTRAST
SURVEILLANCE NEUROIMAGING — KNOWN EGFR-MUTANT NSCLC ON SYSTEMIC THERAPY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
62-year-old female (now age 63) with known Stage IIIB (T3N3M0, upstaged to clinical Stage IV given disease trajectory and prior supraclavicular nodal involvement) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant. Patient has been on osi (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily for approximately 9 months as first-line systemic therapy. Recent CT chest/abdomen/pelvis demonstrates continued partial response with the primary left upper lobe mass now measuring 1.5 cm (down from 4.2 cm at baseline) and minimal residual mediastinal adenopathy. This study is obtained as routine surveillance neuroimaging to evaluate for intracranial metastatic disease, given the known predilection of EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinomas for CNS dissemination and in the context of ongoing systemic therapy response.

COMPARISON:
Prior MRI brain with and without gadolinium obtained at the time of initial staging (approximately 9 months prior). That prior examination was entirely normal with no evidence of intracranial metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE:
Multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3 Tesla scanner. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1 (pre-contrast), axial T2, axial FLAIR (fluid-attenuated inversion recovery), axial diffusion-weighted imaging with corresponding ADC mapping, axial gradient echo susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), axial T1 fat-saturated pre-contrast, and post-gadolinium axial, coronal, and sagittal T1-weighted sequences with fat saturation. Gadobutrol (Gadavist) 0.1 mmol/kg was administered intravenously without complication. Thin-section 1 mm isotropic 3D MPRAGE post-contrast sequences were also obtained for high-sensitivity metastatic disease detection.

FINDINGS:

BRAIN PARENCHYMA:
The cerebral hemispheres are symmetric in configuration. There is no focal area of parenchymal signal abnormality identified on T2 or FLAIR sequences to suggest edema, infarct, contusion, or demyelinating process. The cortical ribbon appears intact bilaterally with appropriate gray-white matter differentiation throughout the frontal, parietal, temporal, and occipital lobes. No areas of restricted diffusion are identified to suggest acute ischemic change.

No intra-axial enhancing lesions are identified on post-gadolinium sequences. Specifically, there are no ring-enhancing or nodular enhancing foci within the cerebral hemispheres, brainstem, cerebellum, or at the gray-white matter junction — the latter being the most common site of hematogenous metastatic implants. The 3D MPRAGE post-contrast sequence, reviewed with both standard windowing and with increased contrast sensitivity settings, does not demonstrate any suspicious foci of abnormal parenchymal enhancement.

FRONTAL LOBES: Unremarkable. No lesions. Normal sulcal pattern without evidence of pachymeningeal or leptomeningeal enhancement.

PARIETAL AND OCCIPITAL LOBES: Normal signal. No parenchymal lesions. No focal enhancement.

TEMPORAL LOBES: Symmetric and unremarkable bilaterally. Hippocampi appear normal in morphology and signal. No mesial temporal signal abnormality.

CEREBELLUM AND BRAINSTEM:
The cerebellar hemispheres are symmetric with normal folial pattern and no mass lesions identified. The cerebellar vermis is midline in position. The brainstem — including the midbrain, pons, and medulla — appears normal in morphology and signal intensity without any focal T2 signal abnormality or abnormal enhancement. The fourth ventricle is patent and normal in configuration.

VENTRICLES AND CISTERNS:
The lateral ventricles are symmetric and mildly prominent bilaterally, within normal limits for patient age, with no evidence of obstruction or transependymal flow to suggest hydrocephalus. The third and fourth ventricles are normal in size and position. The basal cisterns are patent and symmetric. The sylvian fissures are appropriately symmetric.

MENINGES:
No abnormal pachymeningeal or leptomeningeal enhancement identified. Specifically, there is no linear or nodular enhancement along the surface of the brain, within the cerebral sulci, or along the cranial nerves to suggest leptomeningeal carcinomatosis. The dural venous sinuses appear patent on post-contrast sequences.

EXTRA-AXIAL SPACES:
No subdural or epidural collections. No extra-axial fluid collections. The calvarium demonstrates normal marrow signal without focal destructive or infiltrative lesions to suggest osseous metastatic disease. No calvarial erosion or hyperostosis identified.

SELLA AND PARASELLAR REGION:
The pituitary gland is normal in size and morphology. No sellar mass. The cavernous sinuses appear symmetric without evidence of abnormal enhancement or infiltration.

CRANIAL NERVES:
The visualized portions of cranial nerves II through XII appear normal without evidence of perineural tumor spread or abnormal enhancement.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
Major intracranial arterial flow voids are preserved bilaterally including the internal carotid arteries, middle cerebral arteries, anterior cerebral arteries, posterior cerebral arteries, and basilar artery. No dominant flow voids suggesting arteriovenous malformation. No incidental aneurysm identified on this non-dedicated vascular study.

SUSCEPTIBILITY-WEIGHTED IMAGING:
No foci of susceptibility signal loss to suggest hemosiderin deposition, microhemorrhage, or calcification beyond expected physiologic locations (basal ganglia, choroid plexus).

INCIDENTAL FINDINGS:
Small bilateral maxillary sinus mucous retention cysts, noted without significant interval change from prior examination. These are of no clinical significance. No significant mucosal thickening in the visualized paranasal sinuses.

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of intracranial metastatic disease. No enhancing parenchymal lesions identified anywhere within the brain on this dedicated high-sensitivity gadolinium-enhanced examination, including at the gray-white matter junction bilaterally.

2. No leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement to suggest carcinomatous meningitis.

3. Normal brain parenchyma without focal signal abnormality, restricted diffusion, hemorrhage, or mass effect.

4. No significant change from the prior staging MRI brain obtained approximately 9 months ago. Both studies are negative for intracranial disease.

5. Incidental small bilateral maxillary sinus mucous retention cysts, clinically insignificant.

Overall, this is a reassuring surveillance neuroimaging study demonstrating no intracranial involvement in the context of known EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma with ongoing systemic response to osi therapy.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Margaret Chen is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV (upstaged from IIIB at diagnosis, now with adrenal and brain metastases) EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma who has documented systemic and intracranial progression after 14 months of first-line osi 80 mg daily. Her disease course has been notable for an excellent initial partial response -- the primary mass shrank from 4.2 cm down to 1.5 cm, and regional lymphadenopathy substantially resolved -- followed by classic acquired resistance at approximately 14 months, which is consistent with the known median progression-free survival data for Tagrisso in advanced EGFR-mutant NSCLC. She is now showing radiographic evidence of both extracranial (growing primary, new hilar node, new adrenal lesion) and intracranial (three new brain metastases) progression.

She is neurologically intact with an ECOG PS of 1 and remains ambulatory, active, and cognitively sharp. There is no headache, no seizure, no focal deficit, and no signs of elevated intracranial pressure on exam today. This is reassuring and affords us time to conduct appropriate workup before committing to a next systemic therapy.

Key management decisions addressed today:

1. osi -- HELD: osi has been discontinued today given documented progression. There is no role for continuing osi beyond progression in this setting without understanding the resistance mechanism, particularly because the treatment plan will depend heavily on what has driven resistance. Continuing Tagrisso empirically beyond progression in the context of brain metastases is not appropriate here.

2. RESISTANCE MECHANISM PROFILING -- LIQUID BIOPSY ORDERED: A cell-free DNA (cfDNA) plasma-based liquid biopsy has been ordered (Guardant360 or equivalent comprehensive circulating tumor DNA panel) to probe for acquired resistance mutations. The most common mechanism of acquired Tagrisso resistance in the context of the L858R driver mutation would be T790M in-trans acquisition (though T790M is already the basis of Tagrisso's superiority over prior-generation TKIs -- in third-gen TKI setting, C797S trans or cis mutations, MET amplification, KRAS mutations, histologic transformation to small cell carcinoma, and others have been reported). Understanding her specific resistance mechanism will be critical in selecting next therapy. Liquid biopsy results typically return within 7-10 business days.

3. RE-BIOPSY OF RIGHT ADRENAL NODULE: Given the new 1.1 cm right adrenal lesion, we have placed an urgent referral to interventional radiology for CT-guided right adrenal core needle biopsy. This serves two purposes: (a) histologic confirmation that this is indeed a metastatic deposit from her lung adenocarcinoma (and not an adrenal adenoma, which is common incidentally), and (b) tissue-based molecular profiling including comprehensive NGS and specific EGFR resistance mutation testing from this new lesion. We discussed the importance of having fresh tissue from a progressing lesion to best characterize resistance, as primary biopsy specimens are now over 14 months old and may not reflect the current molecular landscape.

4. STEREOTACTIC RADIOSURGERY (SRS) REFERRAL -- URGENT: Given three brain metastases, all measuring 1.6 cm or smaller, with no mass effect and a neurologically intact patient, she is an excellent candidate for stereotactic radiosurgery rather than whole-brain radiotherapy. Preserving neurocognitive function is an important quality-of-life priority, particularly given her excellent performance status and potential for meaningful disease control with appropriate subsequent systemic therapy. An urgent referral has been placed to our radiation oncology colleagues for Gamma Knife or frameless SRS planning. I have spoken to radiation oncology today and they anticipate being able to see her within the week for consultation and to proceed expeditiously with treatment planning. I emphasized the importance of treating the brain lesions while they remain small and asymptomatic.

5. SYSTEMIC THERAPY DECISION -- DEFERRED PENDING WORKUP: We will not commit to a specific next-line systemic regimen until we have the results of the liquid biopsy and the adrenal tissue biopsy/NGS. The decision for next therapy will take into account: (a) the specific resistance mutation identified, (b) any histologic transformation, (c) her overall performance status after SRS recovery, and (d) eligibility for clinical trials. I have already initiated a preliminary search of available clinical trial options at our institution and partner sites for EGFR-mutant NSCLC post-osi progression. We will discuss specific trial opportunities at our next visit once genomic data are available. Platinum-based chemotherapy (carbo + peme) is a standard second-line option for EGFR-mutant adenocarcinoma progressing on an EGFR TKI, and we will certainly revisit that if no better-matched trial is available.

6. DEXAMETHASONE: Given the small size of the brain lesions, lack of edema, and current neurological intactness, I am not initiating prophylactic steroids at this time. If she develops neurological symptoms prior to SRS, she should call our nurse line immediately and we would initiate dexamethasone promptly. She and her husband have been counseled on specific symptoms to watch for (worsening headache, focal weakness, balance problems, new confusion) and instructed to seek emergency evaluation immediately if any of these develop.

7. SUPPORTIVE CARE: Continuing metformin for diabetes management. Lisinopril continued for hypertension. Topical clindamycin and doxycycline may be tapered given that the acneiform rash is now grade 1 and improving -- we will revisit this depending on what next systemic agent is chosen. Paronychia of left index finger is improving, continue warm soaks.

8. RETURN VISIT: I will see Margaret back in the clinic in 1-2 weeks, once liquid biopsy results return and after her radiation oncology consultation, to review all data and finalize next systemic therapy decision. I have asked her to call our nurse triage line immediately for any neurological changes in the interim.

The situation was discussed at length with Margaret and her husband. They understand that this represents a significant disease milestone -- a loss of response to her initial targeted therapy -- but I emphasized that we have additional treatment options, that she is in excellent clinical condition to proceed with further treatment, and that our goal remains to achieve meaningful and durable disease control. They are understandably anxious but remain engaged and motivated. They have questions about what comes next, which we will address more fully once we have the resistance mutation profile in hand.

This mutation is paradoxically the primary resistance mechanism to first-generation TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib) and can emerge as a secondary resistance mechanism during osimertinib therapy through a distinct clonal evolution pathway. This mutation is detected at a lower allele frequency than the primary EGFR driver (19% vs. 44%), consistent with a subclonal or late-acquired resistance mechanism. PIK3CA pathway activation provides bypass signaling that permits tumor cell survival despite EGFR inhibition and may contribute to the compound resistance phenotype observed in this patient.

C797S, which confers resistance to osimertinib by disrupting the covalent bond between osimertinib and the EGFR cysteine residue, is not present above the limit of detection. The absence of C797S in the context of T790M retention is clinically notable, as C797S in cis with T790M would confer triple-mutant resistance (L858R/T790M/C797S) with very limited therapeutic options. The absence of C797S preserves certain therapeutic strategies.

INTERPRETATION AND GENOMIC CONTEXT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

This comprehensive genomic profiling of the right adrenal metastasis was performed in the setting of radiographic progressive disease following approximately 14 months of osimertinib (Tagrisso) therapy for stage IIIB/IV EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma.

The dominant molecular findings are as follows:

1. ON-TARGET EGFR RESISTANCE — EGFR T790M ACQUISITION:
The detection of EGFR p.T790M at a variant allele frequency of 31% represents a high-burden, clonally dominant on-target resistance mechanism to osimertinib. While osimertinib was designed to overcome T790M resistance to first-generation EGFR TKIs, T790M can paradoxically re-emerge during osimertinib therapy through mechanisms that are not completely elucidated but likely involve clonal evolution from a minor pre-existing T790M-containing subclone or de novo acquisition. The T790M substitution replaces the threonine gatekeeper residue with methionine in the EGFR kinase domain, creating steric clash with the osimertinib binding pocket while preserving kinase activity. At 31% allele frequency — approximately 70% of the L858R AF — T790M appears to be present in a substantial proportion of tumor cells, consistent with a primary resistance clone driving progression.

Importantly, no EGFR C797S mutation was detected. C797S, which abolishes osimertinib covalent binding and would preclude use of third-generation TKIs even in combination strategies, is absent. This is relevant because T790M without C797S may retain partial sensitivity to certain fourth-generation EGFR inhibitors currently in clinical development.

2. BYPASS SIGNALING — PIK3CA E545K ACQUISITION:
The newly acquired PIK3CA p.E545K helical domain activating mutation (19% VAF) defines a second, subclonal resistance mechanism. E545K creates constitutive, RAS-independent PI3K activation by disrupting an inhibitory charge interaction between the p110-alpha catalytic subunit and the p85 regulatory subunit. This results in persistent AKT/mTOR signaling independent of EGFR inhibition status, enabling cell survival and proliferation even when EGFR is effectively suppressed. The subclonal nature of this variant (19% vs. 44% for the primary EGFR driver) suggests it may represent a secondary resistance event that accelerated progression or contributed to a more aggressive metastatic phenotype. Concurrent T790M and PIK3CA mutations define a compound resistance profile that may require combinatorial therapeutic strategies targeting both mechanisms.

3. CLONALLY MAINTAINED CO-DRIVERS:
The TP53 p.R175H hotspot mutation (47% VAF), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1/TTF-1 amplification were present at baseline and remain present at this progression specimen, confirming their clonal maintenance throughout disease evolution and treatment exposure. TP53 R175H is a dominant-negative hotspot mutation that abolishes wild-type p53 function and can additionally sequester and inactivate residual wild-type p53, amplifying oncogenic effects. Co-existing TP53 mutation with EGFR-activating mutations is a recognized feature associated with shorter PFS on EGFR TKI therapy in several retrospective datasets. CDKN2A bi-allelic loss eliminates both p16INK4a (CDK4/6 pathway de-repressor) and p14ARF (MDM2 antagonist/p53 stabilizer), further compromising the p53/cell cycle regulatory axis. NKX2-1 amplification is a lineage survival amplification characteristic of lung adenocarcinoma and does not confer TKI resistance per se but may contribute to transcriptional dependencies.

4. ABSENCE OF MET AMPLIFICATION:
MET amplification is one of the most common bypass resistance mechanisms in EGFR-mutant NSCLC, occurring in approximately 15-25% of cases at progression. The absence of MET amplification (copy number 2.1, within diploid range) in this specimen argues against MET-bypass as the operative resistance mechanism and narrows the primary resistance drivers to the on-target T790M mutation and the PIK3CA E545K bypass.

5. ABSENCE OF HISTOLOGIC TRANSFORMATION MARKERS:
No RB1 loss, no neuroendocrine markers (based on gene expression inference), and preservation of TTF-1 amplification are consistent with maintained adenocarcinoma identity without evidence of small cell transformation — a recognized uncommon but clinically important resistance mechanism requiring careful correlation with pathology.

  - Standard platinum-based doublet chemotherapy (e.g., carboplatin + pemetrexed) is an established option for post-EGFR TKI progression in NSCLC.
  - Fourth-generation EGFR inhibitors targeting EGFR with T790M and/or compound resistance mutations (e.g., BLU-945, BBT-176, and related investigational agents) may have activity, particularly in the absence of C797S.
  - Amivantamab (RYBREVANT; EGFR/MET bispecific antibody) combined with platinum-based chemotherapy has demonstrated activity in post-third-generation EGFR TKI progression settings.
  - Osimertinib re-challenge at reduced dose or in combination strategies is under investigation.

  PIK3CA E545K:
  - Alpelisib (Piqray; PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor) has demonstrated activity against PIK3CA-mutant tumors in other histologies and is in clinical development for NSCLC.
  - Combined EGFR inhibitor + PI3K inhibitor strategies are being explored in clinical trials for patients with co-existing EGFR and PIK3CA alterations (PIK3CA inhibition as combination partner to address bypass resistance).
  - Clinical trial enrollment should be strongly considered given the compound resistance profile (EGFR T790M + PIK3CA E545K) that may be specifically addressable by novel combinations.

  TMB-Low / MSS:
  - Anti-PD-1/PD-L1 checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy is not expected to provide substantial benefit based on the low TMB, MSS status, and EGFR-mutant context (multiple datasets demonstrate poor outcomes with ICI monotherapy in EGFR-mutant NSCLC).

1. Spatial heterogeneity: This analysis represents genomic profiling from a single biopsy specimen of a single metastatic site (right adrenal gland). Intratumoral and interlesional genomic heterogeneity may result in other tumor sites (e.g., brain metastases, primary lung mass) harboring different resistance mechanisms not captured in this specimen.

2. Sensitivity limits: Variants present at allele frequencies below 1% may not be reliably detected. A low-level C797S (below limit of detection) could theoretically be present and may emerge with further selective pressure.

3. Epigenetic and transcriptional resistance mechanisms: This panel does not assess epigenetic modifications, promoter methylation, or transcriptional reprogramming (e.g., epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, lineage plasticity) that may contribute to therapeutic resistance.

4. FFPE artifact: Low-frequency variants detected at <5% allele frequency were subjected to additional quality filters to mitigate FFPE-related C>T artifact; ATM and other low-VAF variants listed as VUS were verified as non-artifactual by orthogonal review.

5. Dynamic clonal evolution: The genomic landscape may continue to evolve under ongoing therapeutic selection pressure. Repeat profiling at subsequent progression is recommended.

CLINICAL INDICATION: 63-year-old female with known stage IV non-small cell lung carcinoma, left upper lobe adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, with acquired T790M resistance. Imaging requested for post-SRS treatment response surveillance and evaluation for new intracranial disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the brain was performed on a 3 Tesla magnet using a standard brain tumor protocol. Sequences obtained include: sagittal T1 MPRAGE without gadolinium, axial FLAIR, axial T2, axial diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) with apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) mapping, axial and coronal gradient echo (GRE) susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), and volumetric T1-weighted sequences with gadolinium (Gadavist 0.1 mmol/kg IV administered without adverse reaction). Dedicated thin-section post-contrast sequences through the posterior fossa obtained. Comparison is made to the prior MRI brain dated at the time of progression documentation (approximately 3 months prior) and the MRI brain obtained at the time of initial progression (14 months after osimertinib initiation).

FINDINGS:

Right Frontal SRS Site:
At the site of the previously treated right frontal metastasis, there is a well-circumscribed area of signal abnormality measuring approximately 1.4 cm in greatest dimension, stable in size when compared to the pre-SRS baseline MRI. The lesion demonstrates a heterogeneous pattern of enhancement on post-contrast sequences with a central area of diminished enhancement surrounded by a peripheral irregular enhancing rim, a pattern consistent with evolving post-treatment change. Notably, there is a small central region of restricted diffusion on DWI/ADC sequences, raising the possibility of central coagulative necrosis in keeping with a treatment effect. Surrounding the enhancing focus there is a halo of T2/FLAIR hyperintensity representing vasogenic edema, which appears mildly reduced in extent compared to the prior study. No significant mass effect on the adjacent frontal sulci or gray-white junction. No associated midline shift. The pattern of central low signal on post-gadolinium T1 with surrounding rim enhancement is most consistent with radiation necrosis or maturing treatment effect following SRS; active viable tumor cannot be entirely excluded on conventional MRI sequences alone, and clinical correlation with perfusion imaging or MR spectroscopy may be considered if clinically indicated.

Right Parietal SRS Site:
At the site of the previously treated right parietal metastasis, there is a focal area of enhancement measuring approximately 0.7 cm in greatest dimension, slightly smaller than the 0.8 cm measurement at the time of SRS planning. Enhancement is faint and punctate, less conspicuous than on the prior post-treatment study. The surrounding T2/FLAIR hyperintensity is also diminished compared to earlier imaging. No restricted diffusion. The overall appearance is in keeping with a satisfactory response to radiosurgical treatment, with progressive involution of the treated lesion. No local mass effect or cortical irregularity.

Left Cerebellar Hemisphere SRS Site:
At the site of the previously treated left cerebellar metastasis, a tiny focus of faint enhancement remains visible measuring approximately 0.4 cm, reduced in size from the original 0.6 cm target. This is surrounded by minimal T2 signal change within the adjacent cerebellar white matter. No restricted diffusion. No cerebellar tonsillar descent or mass effect on the fourth ventricle. The appearance is consistent with a favorable treatment response.

Evaluation for New Intracranial Metastases:
Systematic review of all supra- and infratentorial brain parenchyma, leptomeningeal surfaces, cranial nerves, and dural reflections reveals no additional nodular or ring-like areas of abnormal enhancement to suggest new metastatic deposits. In particular, the right and left temporal lobes, bilateral occipital lobes, the brainstem (midbrain, pons, medulla), and the right cerebellar hemisphere are clear of any suspicious signal abnormality or enhancement. No lesion is identified along the tentorium or in the posterior fossa that would be concerning for a new metastasis.

Leptomeningeal Spaces and Ventricular System:
No abnormal leptomeningeal or pachymeningeal enhancement identified. The cortical sulci and basal cisterns remain patent and unremarkable. The ventricular system is within normal limits in size and configuration. No hydrocephalus. No transependymal cerebrospinal fluid signal to suggest elevated intracranial pressure.

Calvarial and Skull Base:
No calvarial or skull base lesion. No evidence of bony metastatic disease at the margins of the imaged field.

Other Intracranial Findings:
There is no evidence of intracranial hemorrhage on gradient echo/SWI sequences aside from a small punctate hemosiderin deposit at the right frontal SRS site, consistent with prior treatment and not representing new hemorrhage. White matter signal changes are mild and scattered in a periventricular and subcortical distribution, nonspecific in appearance and potentially related to chronic small vessel ischemic changes given the patient's history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes. No acute territorial infarct on DWI sequences. Midline structures are in normal position.

IMPRESSION:

1. Stable post-treatment changes at all three prior stereotactic radiosurgery sites (right frontal, right parietal, and left cerebellar hemisphere) compared to the most recent prior MRI. No interval increase in size at any treated site.

2. The right frontal SRS site demonstrates central diminished enhancement with peripheral rim enhancement and restricted diffusion, a pattern most consistent with evolving radiation necrosis or post-treatment effect. This focus has not enlarged and the surrounding vasogenic edema has modestly decreased, favoring treatment response over tumor recurrence. Clinical and metabolic imaging follow-up is suggested at the discretion of the treating team.

5. No leptomeningeal enhancement.

6. Incidental mild periventricular and subcortical white matter T2 signal changes, likely related to small vessel ischemic disease in the setting of hypertension and diabetes; not acutely changed.

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Margaret Chen is a 63-year-old female with stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant non-small cell lung carcinoma (adenocarcinoma) presenting for response assessment following completion of cycle 4 of carboplatin/pemetrexed induction chemotherapy and transition to pemetrexed maintenance. HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Ms. Chen carries a known diagnosis of stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma (left upper lobe primary, with right adrenal metastasis and three brain metastases identified at time of systemic progression). Her disease course to date has been as follows:

She was originally diagnosed with stage IIIB (T3N3M0) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma with bulky mediastinal and ipsilateral supraclavicular lymphadenopathy at age 62. Molecular profiling revealed an EGFR L858R mutation and a co-occurring TP53 R175H mutation, along with CDKN2A homozygous deletion and NKX2-1 amplification. PD-L1 TPS was 15%. TMB was low (4 mut/Mb). She was initiated on osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as definitive systemic therapy, achieving an excellent partial response at 3 months (primary mass 4.2 cm --> 2.1 cm) and a continued deep partial response at 9 months (mass 1.5 cm). She experienced manageable EGFR inhibitor-class toxicities including grade 2 acneiform rash (treated with topical clindamycin and doxycycline) and grade 1 paronychia.

At 14 months on osimertinib, repeat CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed disease progression with growth of the left upper lobe residual mass (1.5 cm --> 2.8 cm), new left hilar lymphadenopathy, and a new 1.1 cm right adrenal nodule. MRI brain simultaneously revealed three new enhancing brain metastases in the right frontal (1.4 cm), right parietal (0.8 cm), and left cerebellar hemispheres (0.6 cm). She was neurologically intact at that time with no symptoms. Osimertinib was held and a CT-guided right adrenal core needle biopsy was performed, which confirmed metastatic adenocarcinoma of lung origin. Repeat NGS on the adrenal specimen demonstrated retention of the EGFR L858R mutation (AF 44%), acquisition of an EGFR T790M resistance mutation (AF 31%), and a newly emergent PIK3CA E545K mutation (AF 19%), consistent with acquired resistance to osimertinib. No EGFR C797S or MET amplification was identified.

She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to all three brain metastases using the Gamma Knife Perfexion platform (right frontal 20 Gy x1, right parietal 20 Gy x1, left cerebellar 18 Gy x1), which she tolerated with grade 2 post-SRS fatigue and grade 1 headache requiring a short dexamethasone course. Following recovery from SRS, she was initiated on second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin (AUC 5) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) every 21 days, with standard folic acid/vitamin B12 supplementation per pemetrexed protocol.

During cycle 2, she developed grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.7 x 10^9/L) with grade 2 nausea, requiring a one-week cycle delay, filgrastim support for 5 days, and antiemetic optimization. She recovered appropriately and was treated at full dose from cycle 3 onward with G-CSF primary prophylaxis. She has now completed 4 cycles total of carboplatin/pemetrexed induction.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Chen is a 63-year-old woman with stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, previously treated with osimertinib (14 months, achieved partial response before acquired T790M/PIK3CA E545K resistance), now on second-line carboplatin/pemetrexed with SRS to oligometastatic brain disease. She is completing her 4-cycle induction course today and is being transitioned to pemetrexed maintenance.

1. Disease Status -- Stable Disease on Carboplatin/Pemetrexed:
Imaging is reassuring. The systemic disease is stable -- the lung primary and adrenal nodule have not grown, and there are no new sites of disease in the chest, abdomen, or pelvis. While we have not achieved a radiographic response (shrinkage) with platinum doublet chemotherapy, disease stabilization in this setting represents a meaningful clinical benefit given the aggressive behavior of this EGFR-mutant tumor at acquired resistance. Intracranially, the SRS results are excellent -- all three treated lesions show post-treatment change consistent with effective ablation, and there are no new brain metastases. Neurologically, she remains completely intact, which is a high priority.

I discussed today's imaging with Ms. Chen and her husband in detail, explaining that the scans show the cancer is "holding steady" on chemotherapy -- it has not grown or spread anywhere new, which is a positive result. I also reassured her that the brain treatment continues to look effective.

2. Transition to Pemetrexed Maintenance:
Given stable disease after 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed induction, she will now transition to pemetrexed (Alimta) maintenance monotherapy, 500 mg/m2 IV every 21 days, per standard of care for non-squamous NSCLC. This is consistent with published data (PARAMOUNT trial) supporting pemetrexed continuation maintenance following platinum doublet induction in the non-squamous setting.

Carboplatin will be discontinued. She will continue:
- Pemetrexed 500 mg/m2 IV every 21 days
- Folic acid 400 mcg daily orally
- Vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM every 9 weeks
- Dexamethasone skin prophylaxis on days -1/0/+1

G-CSF primary prophylaxis: Given prior grade 3 neutropenia on the doublet regimen, I will continue filgrastim 480 mcg SC x5 days starting day 4 post-pemetrexed as a precaution for the initial maintenance cycles, with intent to reassess after cycle 2 of maintenance. Single-agent pemetrexed carries a substantially lower myelosuppression burden than the doublet, and we may be able to de-escalate G-CSF support.

3. Hematologic Monitoring:
Mild anemia (Hgb 10.9 g/dL) is noted -- clinically insignificant at this time, patient is not symptomatic from an anemia standpoint. Will recheck CBC with cycle 1 of maintenance (cycle 5 overall). Iron studies deferred for now unless anemia worsens.

4. Nutritional Status:
Albumin mildly low at 3.6 g/dL. Weight has been stable but mildly reduced from pre-chemo baseline. Husband active in ensuring adequate oral intake at home. I reinforced the importance of caloric density in her diet. Will re-refer to oncology dietitian for a check-in visit. No indication for enteral support at this time.

5. Diabetes / Glucose Management:
Fasting glucose 118 mg/dL, HbA1c 7.1% -- diabetes remains reasonably controlled on metformin. No change to diabetes management at this time. When/if she transitions to investigational therapy in the future (particularly given the PIK3CA E545K resistance mutation, which may make her a candidate for PI3K inhibitor-based clinical trial), hyperglycemia will become an important management concern given PI3K pathway inhibition effects on insulin signaling.

6. CNS Surveillance:
Will continue MRI brain surveillance every 3 months given history of multiple brain metastases and the known CNS propensity of EGFR-mutant NSCLC. Next MRI brain scheduled to coincide with next systemic CT restaging (after 2-3 maintenance cycles).

7. Clinical Trial Consideration -- Future Direction:
I had a frank goals-of-care and treatment planning discussion with Ms. Chen and her husband today. We discussed the trajectory of her disease, acknowledging that while the current chemotherapy regimen is providing disease control, her underlying resistance profile (T790M + PIK3CA E545K co-mutation) is a recognized high-risk biological feature associated with limited durability of platinum-based therapy. I discussed with her that a Phase I/II clinical trial targeting this specific resistance profile (involving a novel EGFR inhibitor and a PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor) may be an option at the time of next progression on pemetrexed maintenance. I have already made contact with the clinical trials team. She expressed strong interest in trial participation if/when she becomes eligible. No formal enrollment or screening initiated at this time.

8. Supportive Care:
- Antiemetics: Continue ondansetron and prochlorperazine PRN. Given that carboplatin (the highly emetogenic component) is now discontinued, overall emetic burden with pemetrexed maintenance should diminish significantly.
- Pain: No pain issues at present.
- Psychosocial: Patient and husband appear to be coping well. She remains engaged and optimistic. Social work follow-up as needed; patient declined formal referral at this time but contact information provided.

9. Follow-up:
She will return in 3 weeks for cycle 1 of pemetrexed maintenance (cycle 5 overall), with pre-cycle CBC, BMP, and LFTs. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain planned after completion of 2-3 maintenance cycles. Oncology dietitian referral placed today.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes, of which more than 50% was counseling and coordination of care.

Margaret Chen is a 64-year-old woman with stage IV (M1c) EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma who presents today for review of her most recent CT imaging, which shows interval disease progression while on pemetrexed maintenance therapy. She was referred for discussion of next-line treatment options and clinical trial eligibility.
