**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**

Ms. Chen is a 64-year-old retired accountant and former smoker (25 pack-years, quit approximately 10 years ago) with a known history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Her oncologic history is detailed below.

She was originally diagnosed approximately 2 years ago with stage IIIB (T3N3M0, AJCC 8th edition) left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma following evaluation of an incidental left upper lobe mass and subsequent CT-guided core needle biopsy. Molecular profiling at that time (NGS from the primary tumor) demonstrated an EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation, TP53 p.R175H co-mutation, CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification, with no KRAS, MET, ALK, ROS1, RET, or ERBB2 alterations. TMB was low (4 mut/Mb). PD-L1 TPS was 15%.

Given the sensitizing EGFR mutation and unresectable N3 disease, a multidisciplinary tumor board recommended upfront EGFR-directed therapy rather than standard chemoradiation. She was started on osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally daily as definitive first-line therapy. She achieved an excellent initial partial response, with the primary mass shrinking from 4.2 cm to a nadir of 1.5 cm at 9 months, with near-complete resolution of mediastinal and supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. She tolerated osimertinib reasonably well, experiencing grade 2 acneiform rash managed with topical clindamycin and oral doxycycline, as well as grade 1 paronychia of the left index finger treated with warm soaks.

Unfortunately, at approximately 14 months into osimertinib therapy, she developed radiographic progression with an increase in the left upper lobe mass (1.5 cm to 2.8 cm), new left hilar adenopathy, and a new right adrenal nodule. Concurrent brain MRI revealed three new enhancing brain metastases (right frontal 1.4 cm, right parietal 0.8 cm, left cerebellar 0.6 cm), upstaging her disease to stage IV M1c. She was neurologically intact throughout. CT-guided biopsy of the right adrenal metastasis confirmed metastatic lung adenocarcinoma; repeat NGS on the adrenal specimen revealed acquisition of an EGFR T790M (exon 20) resistance mutation (AF 31%) as well as a new PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (AF 19%), on the background of persistent EGFR L858R, TP53 R175H, CDKN2A deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification.

She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to all three brain metastases using the Gamma Knife Perfexion platform (right frontal: 20 Gy x1, right parietal: 20 Gy x1, left cerebellar: 18 Gy x1) approximately 3 weeks after progression was documented. She had anticipated post-SRS grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 headache, managed with a short dexamethasone course. Whole-brain radiation was deferred in favor of SRS to preserve neurocognitive function.

Osimertinib was discontinued given clear acquired resistance. Second-line systemic therapy with carboplatin (AUC 5 IV day 1) plus pemetrexed (500 mg/m² IV day 1) every 21 days was initiated approximately 6 weeks after progression documentation, once she had recovered from SRS. Vitamin B12, folic acid, and dexamethasone premedication were given per standard pemetrexed protocol. Cycle 2 was complicated by grade 3 neutropenia (ANC 0.7 x 10⁹/L), requiring a 1-week treatment delay and a 5-day course of filgrastim. G-CSF prophylaxis was added for all subsequent cycles. She completed a total of 4 cycles of carboplatin/pemetrexed induction with best systemic response of stable disease (primary mass stable at 2.8 cm, adrenal nodule stable at 1.1 cm, no new systemic lesions). Brain MRI after 3 cycles showed stable post-SRS treatment effect at all three prior sites with no new intracranial disease.

Following completion of 4 cycles of induction, she transitioned to pemetrexed maintenance (500 mg/m² IV every 21 days) with continued vitamin B12 and folic acid supplementation. She tolerated maintenance reasonably well without significant new toxicities.

Her most recent staging CT (performed approximately 3 weeks ago, after completing 3 cycles of pemetrexed maintenance) has now demonstrated interval disease progression, which is the primary impetus for today's visit. Imaging showed enlargement of the left upper lobe mass from 2.8 cm to 4.0 cm, growth of the right adrenal nodule from 1.1 cm to 2.0 cm, a new hepatic lesion in segment VI measuring 0.9 cm (new from prior), and a new small right-sided pleural effusion. These findings are consistent with clear systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance.

Ms. Chen reports she has been feeling more fatigued over the past 4-6 weeks, rating energy at roughly 5-6/10. She notes mild right-sided chest discomfort, which she attributes to the pleural effusion — she describes it as a dull ache rather than pleuritic pain, not significantly limiting her activity. She denies shortness of breath at rest and reports only mild exertional dyspnea when climbing one flight of stairs. She has not noted any abdominal pain or right upper quadrant discomfort referable to the hepatic lesion. She remains neurologically intact: no headaches, no visual changes, no focal weakness, no gait difficulties, no seizure activity. She continues her home medications including metformin for her type 2 diabetes and amlodipine and lisinopril for hypertension, all at stable doses.

She lives with her husband, who is actively involved in her care and accompanied her today. She maintains a good appetite with slight weight loss (~2 lbs over the past month), which she attributes to reduced appetite. She has been researching clinical trial options and came to this visit prepared to discuss enrollment.

**ASSESSMENT AND PLAN**

Ms. Chen is a 64-year-old woman with stage IV EGFR L858R-mutant left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, now with acquired EGFR T790M resistance and a co-acquired PIK3CA E545K activating mutation, presenting with clear radiographic evidence of systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance therapy — her third line of treatment overall. Disease burden has expanded to include enlarging pulmonary primary, enlarging adrenal metastasis, a new hepatic lesion, and a new small pleural effusion. Intracranial disease remains controlled per prior brain MRI at last assessment, with stable post-SRS treatment changes and no new brain metastases identified; pending today's new MRI brain for confirmation.

**Problem 1: Systemic progression on pemetrexed maintenance — transition to next-line therapy**

Her molecular profile, specifically the co-existence of an acquired EGFR T790M resistance mutation alongside a PIK3CA E545K activating mutation, places her in a biologically distinct and clinically challenging resistance population. Standard third-line options for EGFR-mutant NSCLC after platinum-based chemotherapy are limited; her prior T790M-mediated resistance to osimertinib, which would have otherwise been a logical choice in the platinum-naive T790M+ setting, complicates straightforward re-challenge strategies. Given the PIK3CA co-mutation, PI3K pathway activation is a plausible driver of both the acquired osimertinib resistance and the relative modest response to platinum doublet chemotherapy.

Following a thorough discussion of available options — including docetaxel monotherapy, docetaxel/ramucirumab, further clinical trial enrollment, or best supportive care — and having reviewed her prior treatment history, molecular profile, and current performance status, I discussed in detail the availability of a Phase I/II clinical trial (protocol NCT-XXXXXX) evaluating a novel combination of an investigational EGFR inhibitor designed to overcome T790M/C797S resistance and a PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor (alpelisib analog) in patients with EGFR-mutant NSCLC harboring acquired PIK3CA co-mutations. Ms. Chen has been made aware of the investigational nature of this therapy, the relevant risks including hyperglycemia (expected class effect of PI3K inhibition), potential for rash, mucositis, diarrhea, and hepatotoxicity, as well as uncertainty about efficacy.

Ms. Chen has expressed strong interest in clinical trial enrollment. She has reviewed the consent form with me and with her husband today, asked thoughtful questions, and signed the informed consent document. She meets eligibility criteria based on her molecular profile (EGFR L858R + T790M + PIK3CA E545K), prior therapy history, ECOG PS 1-2, and adequate organ function as confirmed by today's labs.

Plan:
- Pemetrexed maintenance: DISCONTINUED as of today.
- Enroll on Phase I/II trial, investigational EGFR inhibitor + PI3K inhibitor combination arm. Cycle 1 Day 1 to be scheduled at the trial site within 1-2 weeks, pending confirmation of baseline trial assessments.
- Baseline trial assessments to complete prior to C1D1: fasting glucose, HbA1c, LDH, hepatic function panel (already obtained this week — confirm values meet eligibility thresholds), ECG (QTc 438 ms on most recent ECG from pemetrexed cycle — acceptable; will recheck at baseline), ophthalmologic exam (trial requirement for PI3K inhibitor), and formal ECOG PS documentation.
- Vitamin B12 and folic acid: Will continue per trial protocol requirements given prior pemetrexed use.
- Metformin: Continue at current dose (1000 mg BID); diabetes management will be critically important given anticipated PI3K inhibitor-related hyperglycemia. Patient counseled extensively on dietary modification, low-carbohydrate diet, glucose monitoring at home, and when to call the clinic. Close coordination with her primary care physician and endocrinologist if needed.

**Problem 2: Brain metastases — surveillance**

MRI brain ordered today to confirm ongoing intracranial control prior to trial enrollment. Results pending; patient instructed to call with any neurological symptoms (headache, visual changes, confusion, weakness) and told we will call her with the MRI results, likely within 24-48 hours. If new or enlarging brain metastases are identified, urgent neurosurgery/radiation oncology co-management will be arranged. Trial eligibility requires stable CNS disease (no new or symptomatic brain metastases requiring urgent intervention). Prior SRS sites expected to show stable or evolving radiation changes.

**Problem 3: New right-sided pleural effusion — monitoring**

Small right-sided pleural effusion noted on CT. Patient is not dyspneic at rest, SpO2 95% on room air, and the effusion appears small on imaging. No thoracentesis needed at this time. Will monitor clinically; if she develops worsening dyspnea, decreased saturation, or the effusion enlarges on serial imaging, thoracentesis may be considered for both symptomatic relief and cytological confirmation of malignant etiology.

**Problem 4: Mild anemia**

Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL, consistent with chronic anemia of cancer/chemotherapy. Not symptomatic enough to warrant transfusion at this time. Will recheck CBC in the context of trial enrollment baseline labs. If further decline or symptomatic anemia (dyspnea at rest, fatigue significantly limiting activity), will revisit transfusion and/or ESA options per trial protocol restrictions.

**Problem 5: Mildly elevated hepatic transaminases**

AST 32 and ALT 38 (both at or just above the upper limit of normal), with mildly elevated alkaline phosphatase at 112. New hepatic lesion in segment VI raises the question of whether hepatic infiltration is contributing. Will monitor closely at trial baseline and subsequent visits. If significant worsening, hepatology consultation and trial eligibility re-assessment may be needed.

**Problem 6: Hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes — stable comorbidities**

BP well controlled today at 128/76. Fasting glucose 138 mg/dL, acceptable for now but glucose management will become a priority once PI3K inhibitor is initiated. Coordination with PCP requested. Will check HbA1c at trial baseline.

INDICATION: Staging of newly diagnosed left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma. EGFR L858R mutation detected. Evaluate for extent of locoregional and distant disease.

TECHNIQUE: Whole body PET-CT was performed following intravenous administration of approximately 12.5 mCi of F-18 fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG). The patient fasted for a minimum of 6 hours prior to injection. Serum glucose at time of injection was 108 mg/dL. Images were acquired from the vertex of the skull to the mid-thighs approximately 60 minutes post-injection using a combined PET-CT scanner. Diagnostic-quality CT images of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis were obtained with intravenous contrast. Attenuation-corrected PET images were acquired and fused with CT. Non-attenuation-corrected images were reviewed for potential artifactual findings. Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes.

COMPARISON: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed recently (see prior report).

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
There is no abnormal FDG uptake in the brain parenchyma beyond expected physiologic cortical activity. No hypermetabolic intracranial lesions identified. There is no pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid cervical lymphadenopathy. The salivary glands, thyroid gland, and visualized soft tissues of the neck are unremarkable. Physiologic symmetric FDG uptake is noted in the palatine tonsils and base of tongue.

A cluster of left-sided supraclavicular/lower cervical lymph nodes demonstrates markedly elevated FDG uptake (SUVmax 8.7), corresponding to confluent soft tissue density lymph nodes measuring up to approximately 1.8 cm on the CT component. These nodes appear borderline in size but their avidity is clearly pathologic and disproportionate to any reactive process. There is no corresponding right supraclavicular nodal activity.

CHEST:
Primary Lesion: There is an intensely hypermetabolic, irregular, spiculated mass in the left upper lobe demonstrating markedly elevated FDG uptake (SUVmax 12.4). On the CT component, the mass measures approximately 4.2 cm in greatest dimension, with spiculated margins, and abuts but does not appear to clearly invade the adjacent pleura. The lesion demonstrates heterogeneous internal attenuation, with areas of central hypometabolism potentially reflecting focal necrosis. The degree of FDG avidity is clearly consistent with aggressive primary malignancy.

Mediastinum and Hilar Regions: There is abnormal FDG uptake in multiple mediastinal lymph node stations. Specifically:
- Left paratracheal/aortopulmonary window (stations 5 and 6): Mildly enlarged lymph nodes with elevated FDG uptake, SUVmax approximately 7.1. The aortopulmonary window nodes measure up to 1.4 cm on CT and show clearly pathologic metabolic activity.
- Left lower paratracheal (station 4L): A lymph node cluster demonstrating FDG avidity with SUVmax approximately 6.9, measuring up to 1.2 cm on CT.
- No significant right-sided mediastinal or right hilar nodal activity. The subcarinal space (station 7) does not demonstrate convincingly pathologic FDG uptake; a few small nodes are present but are below the threshold for confident characterization.
- The left hilum itself shows mildly increased activity (SUVmax approximately 5.4) without discrete masslike hilar adenopathy, though differentiation from the primary mass hilar extension is limited.

Pleura: No pleural FDG uptake to suggest pleural involvement. No pleural effusion.

Lungs: No additional pulmonary nodules or masses with FDG uptake to suggest intrapulmonary metastases. A few sub-centimeter ground-glass nodules are noted in the right lower lobe on CT without FDG activity, too small to characterize by PET; likely incidental/benign given lack of avidity.

Heart and Pericardium: Physiologic myocardial FDG uptake pattern. No pericardial abnormality.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and demonstrates homogeneous physiologic FDG uptake throughout. No focal hepatic lesions with elevated FDG activity. No biliary ductal dilation.

Spleen: Normal size. No splenic lesions. Physiologic splenic FDG uptake, not exceeding hepatic background activity.

Pancreas: Unremarkable. No pancreatic FDG avidity.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in morphology on CT without enlargement or nodularity. No FDG uptake above hepatic background activity in either adrenal gland. The adrenal glands are not suspicious for metastatic involvement.

Kidneys and Ureters: Normal renal size and morphology. Physiologic tracer excretion into the collecting systems bilaterally. No renal or perirenal FDG-avid lesions.

Bowel and Mesentery: Physiologic and scattered FDG activity throughout the gastrointestinal tract, consistent with expected bowel activity. No focal areas of pathologic uptake to suggest serosal or mesenteric implants.

Retroperitoneum: No retroperitoneal or para-aortic lymphadenopathy with pathologic FDG uptake.

PELVIS:
No pelvic organ abnormalities. Uterus and adnexa are not enlarged. No pathologic pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No FDG-avid pelvic lesions. Physiologic bladder activity from FDG excretion.

SKELETAL SURVEY:
The osseous structures are evaluated throughout the field of view including skull base, cervical spine, thoracic and lumbar spine, pelvis, proximal femora, and visualized ribs and sternum. There is no focal FDG uptake in the skeletal system above background to suggest osseous metastatic disease. No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified on the CT component. The vertebral bodies demonstrate normal trabeculae and marrow signal equivalent on non-contrast CT without morphologic evidence of bony involvement.

SOFT TISSUES:
No subcutaneous or intramuscular FDG-avid lesions. No soft tissue masses. Physiologic muscle uptake in expected distribution.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. INTENSELY FDG-AVID LEFT UPPER LOBE MASS (SUVmax 12.4): The primary left upper lobe lung mass demonstrates markedly elevated metabolic activity entirely consistent with aggressive primary lung malignancy, in keeping with the known diagnosis of adenocarcinoma. The degree of avidity further supports high-grade biological behavior.

2. HYPERMETABOLIC MEDIASTINAL LYMPHADENOPATHY: FDG-avid nodal disease in the left paratracheal region (station 4L), aortopulmonary window (station 5), and subaortic region (station 6) confirming locoregional mediastinal spread. The metabolic activity in these stations is clearly above any threshold attributable to reactive/inflammatory change.

3. HYPERMETABOLIC LEFT SUPRACLAVICULAR LYMPHADENOPATHY (SUVmax 8.7): Pathologically FDG-avid left supraclavicular/low cervical nodal cluster representing N3 disease, a key determinant in the overall stage classification. This finding is consistent with the left supraclavicular adenopathy identified on the prior contrast-enhanced CT.

4. NO EVIDENCE OF DISTANT METASTATIC DISEASE: Thorough evaluation of the liver, adrenal glands, kidneys, peritoneum, and retroperitoneum demonstrates no FDG-avid lesions to suggest visceral metastases. The skeletal survey is negative for osseous metastatic involvement. No distant organ sites show suspicious hypermetabolism.

5. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: The PET-CT findings are compatible with locoregionally advanced left-sided lung adenocarcinoma with ipsilateral mediastinal nodal involvement and left supraclavicular (N3) adenopathy, without evidence of distant metastatic spread. These findings, integrated with the recently performed contrast-enhanced CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis, are consistent with stage IIIB (T3 N3 M0) disease per AJCC 8th edition staging criteria.

No incidental FDG-avid findings of clinical concern outside of the known primary malignancy and regional lymph node disease.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Chen is a 64-year-old woman with a well-characterized history of left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, initially diagnosed approximately 2 years ago as Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) disease harboring an EGFR exon 21 L858R activating mutation (allele frequency 38%) with co-occurring TP53 R175H and CDKN2A homozygous deletion on baseline NGS. She achieved a durable partial response on first-line osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily, with the left upper lobe primary shrinking from 4.2 cm down to 1.5 cm at nadir over approximately 9 months of therapy. Unfortunately, she progressed at the 14-month mark with both systemic progression (enlarging left upper lobe mass, new hilar adenopathy, new right adrenal nodule) and simultaneous development of three new brain metastases identified on MRI (right frontal 1.4 cm, right parietal 0.8 cm, left cerebellar 0.6 cm). Repeat NGS from the right adrenal biopsy confirmed acquisition of EGFR T790M (allele frequency 31%) as a canonical osimertinib resistance mechanism, as well as a newly acquired PIK3CA E545K mutation (allele frequency 19%), which has been recognized increasingly as a co-resistance driver in EGFR-mutant NSCLC.

Following osimertinib discontinuation, she underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) via Gamma Knife Perfexion to all three brain metastases (right frontal 20 Gy/1 fraction, right parietal 20 Gy/1 fraction, left cerebellar 18 Gy/1 fraction). She tolerated SRS well with expected transient grade 2 fatigue and grade 1 headache that resolved after a brief dexamethasone taper. The trial combines an investigational EGFR inhibitor with activity against T790M/C797S-resistant conformations with a PI3K alpha-selective inhibitor (alpelisib analog), targeting both identified resistance pathways simultaneously. She is currently in the dose expansion cohort and began Cycle 1 Day 1 approximately 8 weeks ago.

She was already on metformin at baseline for her type 2 diabetes mellitus; the PI3K inhibitor component of the trial predictably exacerbated her hyperglycemia, which has been a recognized on-target toxicity of this class.

The sections show cores of lung parenchyma extensively replaced and infiltrated by a malignant epithelial neoplasm. Desmoplastic stromal reaction is prominent, with dense fibrotic stroma surrounding the neoplastic glands. Focal areas of necrosis are present within the solid component. Perineural invasion is identified in one core. Lymphovascular invasion is focally present and suspicious in two cores. No sarcomatoid or pleomorphic differentiation is identified.

COMMENT:
The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings are diagnostic of invasive adenocarcinoma of the lung, acinar predominant pattern with a significant solid component. The solid component (40%) confers poorly differentiated (Grade 3) status per current IASLC/WHO classification. The immunophenotype strongly supports a primary pulmonary origin; metastatic adenocarcinoma from extrapulmonary sites (breast, gastrointestinal, renal) is effectively excluded by the TTF-1 and Napsin-A positivity in this clinical context.

A sensitizing EGFR exon 21 L858R point mutation has been confirmed by PCR-based testing on this specimen, a finding of direct therapeutic relevance. PD-L1 TPS of 15% falls in the low positive category. ALK and ROS1 rearrangements are not identified by IHC and FISH respectively. Comprehensive NGS is pending and a supplemental report will be issued.

Specimen adequacy is confirmed as satisfactory for molecular profiling purposes based on tumor cellularity and nucleic acid yield from extracted material.

Correlation with clinical, radiologic, and other laboratory findings is recommended.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. EGFR p.L858R (c.2573T>G) — Exon 21
   Classification: Pathogenic / Activating — TIER IA (Strong Clinical Significance)
   Variant Type: Single nucleotide variant (missense)
   Allele Frequency: 38%
   Functional Impact: The L858R substitution in exon 21 of the EGFR kinase domain is one of the two most common sensitizing EGFR mutations in non-small cell lung adenocarcinoma, accounting for approximately 40–45% of all EGFR-activating mutations. This missense change (leucine to arginine at codon 858) within the activation loop of the kinase domain leads to constitutive receptor activation independent of ligand binding, driving downstream RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling and tumor proliferation. The measured allele frequency of 38% is consistent with a heterozygous somatic mutation given the estimated 55% tumor cellularity of this specimen.
   Clinical Significance: EGFR L858R is a well-established predictive biomarker for response to EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitors (TKIs). Osimertinib (TAGRISSO), a third-generation, CNS-penetrant, irreversible EGFR TKI, is the standard first-line therapy for advanced/metastatic EGFR-mutant NSCLC (per FLAURA trial data) and is being evaluated in earlier-stage settings. Osimertinib's superiority over first-generation TKIs (gefitinib, erlotinib) and second-generation TKIs (afatinib, dacomitinib) in terms of PFS, OS, and CNS control has been demonstrated. First-generation TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib) and second-generation TKIs (afatinib) also demonstrate activity against L858R, though with inferior CNS penetration and a higher rate of acquired T790M resistance (~50% of cases). Notably, EGFR exon 19 deletions have slightly superior outcomes to L858R mutations with TKI therapy in some analyses, though both are considered sensitizing mutations warranting TKI therapy.

2. TP53 p.R175H (c.524G>A) — Exon 5
   Classification: Pathogenic — TIER IA (Prognostic Significance; no approved targeted therapy)
   Variant Type: Single nucleotide variant (missense)
   Allele Frequency: 42%
   Functional Impact: TP53 encodes p53, the canonical tumor suppressor and "guardian of the genome." The p.R175H substitution is one of the six most frequent "hotspot" gain-of-function TP53 mutations, occurring at an arginine residue critical for the structural integrity of the DNA-binding domain. Unlike null/truncating TP53 mutations that simply abrogate tumor suppression, the R175H variant confers dominant-negative inhibition of wild-type p53 activity and acquires neomorphic oncogenic gain-of-function properties, including promotion of epithelial-mesenchymal transition, increased genomic instability, and enhanced resistance to cellular stress. The allele frequency of 42% is consistent with a clonal, likely early-truncal somatic event in this tumor. TP53 co-mutation is observed in approximately 50–60% of EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinomas.
   Clinical Significance: TP53 co-mutation in EGFR-mutant NSCLC has been associated with shorter progression-free survival on first-generation EGFR TKIs in some retrospective series, with conflicting data regarding impact on osimertinib outcomes. Concurrent TP53 R175H may contribute to increased genomic instability and potential for clonal evolution during therapy. There are no currently approved therapies specifically targeting TP53 R175H, though several investigational approaches (APR-246/eprenetapopt targeting p53 R175H refolding, and PRIMA-1MET) are in clinical development. This mutation should be flagged for monitoring during treatment; its persistence and clonal dynamics on serial liquid biopsy could provide prognostic information.

3. CDKN2A — Homozygous Deletion
   Classification: Pathogenic — TIER IIA (Clinically Significant)
   Variant Type: Copy number loss (homozygous deletion of chromosome 9p21.3)
   Functional Impact: CDKN2A encodes p16/INK4A (and also p14/ARF from an alternative reading frame), a critical inhibitor of CDK4/6 that prevents CDK4/6-mediated phosphorylation of retinoblastoma protein (RB1) and cell cycle progression through G1/S. Homozygous deletion, as observed here, results in complete loss of p16 function, rendering CDK4/6 constitutively active and bypassing G1/S checkpoint control. Homozygous CDKN2A deletion is detected in approximately 25–35% of lung adenocarcinomas and is considered a marker of more aggressive tumor biology. Co-occurrence with EGFR mutations has been described, and some analyses suggest CDKN2A deletion may be associated with shorter time to osimertinib resistance. This finding also formally explains the absence of p16 expression on immunohistochemistry that may be noted in this tumor.
   Clinical Significance: CDK4/6 inhibitors (e.g., palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) have limited evidence in NSCLC with CDKN2A deletion, though combination strategies are under investigation. CDKN2A deletion is primarily a prognostic marker in this context. It does not preclude EGFR TKI therapy.

4. NKX2-1 (TTF-1) — High-Level Amplification
   Classification: Likely Pathogenic / Oncogenic — TIER IIB
   Variant Type: Copy number gain (focal amplification, chromosome 14q13.3)
   Estimated Copy Number: ≥8 copies (high-level amplification)
   Functional Impact: NKX2-1, encoding the transcription factor TTF-1 (Thyroid Transcription Factor-1), is a lineage-specific oncogene in lung adenocarcinoma. TTF-1 protein expression (by IHC) was confirmed in this tumor, consistent with the amplification detected at the genomic level. NKX2-1 amplification promotes tumor cell survival through lineage-survival oncogene addiction and has been associated with upregulation of the RAS/ERK axis, activation of FNBP1L, and modulation of MAPK signaling. NKX2-1 amplification is observed in approximately 10–15% of lung adenocarcinomas and is essentially specific to this histologic subtype.
   Clinical Significance: NKX2-1 amplification is a lineage-specific oncogenic event without a directly targetable approved therapy at this time. Its co-occurrence with EGFR L858R suggests convergent oncogenic signaling via multiple pathways. NKX2-1 amplification may modulate sensitivity to EGFR-directed therapy, though clinical data remain limited. This finding further supports the primary lung adenocarcinoma histology and may be useful as a clonal marker on future liquid biopsies.

GENOMIC CONTEXT AND CLONAL ARCHITECTURE INTERPRETATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
The mutational profile of this tumor demonstrates a clonal EGFR L858R mutation (AF 38%) co-occurring with a similarly clonal TP53 p.R175H mutation (AF 42%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1 amplification. The comparable allele frequencies of the EGFR and TP53 mutations suggest that both are early truncal events present in the predominant tumor clone, rather than representing distinct subclonal populations. This is consistent with published phylogenetic analyses of EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma, in which TP53 co-mutation is commonly identified as a co-truncal event arising prior to or concurrent with EGFR mutation acquisition.

The CDKN2A homozygous deletion, by contrast, may represent a secondary truncal alteration or an early subclonal event that has expanded to clonal dominance. In the context of an EGFR-mutant / TP53-co-mutant background, CDKN2A deletion further deregulates cell cycle control and may contribute to the poorly differentiated (grade 3) histology observed in this tumor.

The NKX2-1 amplification serves as a lineage identity marker and is consistent with the TTF-1-positive, Napsin-A-positive immunophenotype confirmed on prior pathology. It is not expected to confound interpretation of EGFR-directed therapy.

The absence of T790M at baseline is expected and important to document, as T790M is an acquired resistance mechanism that emerges under selective pressure of EGFR TKI therapy in approximately 40–60% of cases treated with first- or second-generation TKIs and, less commonly, under osimertinib. Baseline ctDNA/liquid biopsy profiling at the time of clinical progression is recommended to assess for emergence of T790M or alternative resistance mechanisms.

This assay employs hybrid capture-based next-generation sequencing of a comprehensive panel of >500 cancer-relevant genes for detection of single nucleotide variants (SNVs), short insertions and deletions (indels), copy number variations (CNVs), and select structural rearrangements/fusions. Parallel RNA sequencing is performed for fusion transcript detection (ALK, ROS1, RET, NTRK1/2/3, MET, and others). Tumor mutational burden (TMB) is calculated from the somatic SNV and indel count across the coding regions of the panel, normalized to megabase pairs of coding sequence assessed. Microsatellite instability (MSI) is assessed by fragment analysis of >2,000 microsatellite loci. Pathogenic germline variants identified incidentally through this somatic profiling assay were evaluated against the germline comparator; no reportable pathogenic germline variants were identified in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, or other actionable cancer predisposition genes.

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Chen is a 62-year-old female with a newly diagnosed left upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma, stage IIIB (T3N3M0), referred for oncologic evaluation and treatment planning. She presents today in the context of a formal multidisciplinary thoracic tumor board discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Chen is a 62-year-old retired accountant and former smoker (25 pack-years, quit approximately 8 years ago) who initially presented to her primary care physician approximately 6 weeks ago with a several-week history of progressive left-sided chest discomfort, mild exertional dyspnea, and a persistent, non-productive cough. She denied hemoptysis at that time. A chest X-ray was obtained, which identified an abnormal opacity in the left upper lobe, prompting expedited referral for CT imaging.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated a 4.2 cm spiculated left upper lobe mass with associated mediastinal lymphadenopathy at stations 4L, 5, and 6, as well as left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusion was identified. No evidence of hepatic, adrenal, or osseous metastatic disease was seen. Whole-body PET-CT confirmed FDG-avidity of the primary mass (SUVmax 12.4) and the mediastinal and supraclavicular nodal disease (SUVmax up to 8.7), with no evidence of distant organ or skeletal metastatic involvement. Brain MRI with and without gadolinium demonstrated no intracranial metastatic disease and no leptomeningeal enhancement.

A CT-guided core needle biopsy of the left upper lobe mass was performed and confirmed invasive adenocarcinoma, acinar predominant (approximately 60%), with solid components (approximately 40%). Immunohistochemistry was positive for TTF-1 and Napsin-A, consistent with primary pulmonary origin. Tumor grade is poorly differentiated (Grade 3).

Molecular profiling by next-generation sequencing (NGS) on the biopsy specimen identified an EGFR p.L858R activating mutation (exon 21 point mutation) with an allele frequency of 38%, a co-existing TP53 p.R175H missense mutation (VAF 42%), CDKN2A homozygous deletion, and NKX2-1 (TTF-1) amplification. No KRAS, NRAS, BRAF, MET, RET, ROS1, ALK, ERBB2, or FGFR alterations were detected. No baseline T790M resistance mutation was identified. TMB was low at 4 mutations/Mb; MSI-stable. PD-L1 TPS by 22C3 assay was 15%.

Final staging: Stage IIIB (T3N3M0, AJCC 8th edition) non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, left upper lobe, with EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation.

The case was presented formally at today's thoracic oncology multidisciplinary tumor board attended by thoracic oncology, radiation oncology, thoracic surgery, interventional radiology, and diagnostic radiology.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION SUMMARY

Ms. Chen's case was presented today at the thoracic oncology multidisciplinary tumor board with participation from medical oncology (thoracic), radiation oncology, thoracic surgery, and diagnostic radiology.

Key points of discussion:

1. Staging: The board confirmed stage IIIB (T3N3M0) disease. The N3 designation reflects ipsilateral supraclavicular lymph node involvement (station 1), which by AJCC 8th edition criteria represents contralateral or scalene/supraclavicular disease. This is technically resectable in anatomy but is universally considered unresectable from an oncologic standpoint due to the extent of nodal disease. Thoracic surgery confirmed that surgical resection is not an appropriate option for this patient.

2. Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy (CCRT): Standard of care for unresectable stage III NSCLC without targetable mutations is concurrent platinum-based chemotherapy with thoracic radiotherapy followed by durvalumab consolidation (per the PACIFIC trial paradigm). However, this patient harbors a sensitizing EGFR L858R mutation, which substantially changes the management algorithm.

3. Role of EGFR-targeted therapy in stage III disease: There is growing evidence — including prospective data from the LAURA trial (osimertinib after CCRT in EGFR-mutant stage III NSCLC) and retrospective cohort data — supporting the use of EGFR TKI therapy in the definitive setting for EGFR-mutant stage III disease. In EGFR-mutant tumors, immunotherapy with durvalumab has not demonstrated clear benefit (and some data suggest potential inferior outcomes or excess toxicity with TKIs following immunotherapy). The tumor board consensus was that given the confirmed EGFR L858R sensitizing mutation, this patient is best served by upfront EGFR-directed systemic therapy rather than the standard CCRT/durvalumab pathway.

4. Choice of EGFR TKI: Osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily is the preferred third-generation, CNS-penetrant EGFR TKI for EGFR-mutant NSCLC. Its superior efficacy data compared to first-generation TKIs (FLAURA trial), CNS activity, and favorable tolerability profile make it the agent of choice. The absence of a baseline T790M mutation does not preclude use; osimertinib is active against EGFR L858R regardless. The TP53 co-mutation is noted as a potentially adverse prognostic feature, as co-occurring TP53 mutations in EGFR-mutant NSCLC have been associated with shorter PFS in some series, but this does not change the first-line recommendation.

5. Radiation oncology input: Radiation oncology evaluated the patient. While definitive thoracic radiotherapy is not being pursued upfront as the primary treatment modality, the radiation oncologist will remain engaged in this patient's care. Potential roles for radiation include: (a) consolidation thoracic radiotherapy after achievement of a robust systemic response to osimertinib, particularly if residual mediastinal disease can be rendered to a manageable volume; (b) management of any sites of symptomatic or oligoprogressive disease should that occur in the future.

6. Regarding PD-L1 and immunotherapy: PD-L1 TPS is 15%, which would not qualify for single-agent pembrolizumab first-line (requires TPS ≥50% for monotherapy per KEYNOTE-024) and in any event, immune checkpoint inhibitors are generally deferred in EGFR-mutant NSCLC given lack of benefit and potential for increased toxicity in combination or sequential use with EGFR TKIs.

TUMOR BOARD CONSENSUS DECISION: Initiate osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg orally once daily as first-line definitive systemic therapy for this patient's stage IIIB EGFR L858R-mutant lung adenocarcinoma. Radiation oncology to remain involved and re-evaluate for potential consolidation radiotherapy once systemic response is assessed at approximately 3 months. Baseline ECG to confirm acceptable QTc prior to starting therapy.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. Stage IIIB (T3N3M0) non-small cell lung carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, left upper lobe, EGFR L858R-mutant, PD-L1 TPS 15%, TP53 co-mutant, CDKN2A deleted:
   - Disease is unresectable given N3 (supraclavicular) nodal involvement; surgical resection not planned
   - Tumor board consensus to initiate osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily; intent is curative/definitive given locoregional disease without distant metastases
   - Baseline ECG obtained today: QTc 418 ms, acceptable; no contraindication to osimertinib from a cardiac standpoint
   - Patient counseled extensively regarding osimertinib mechanism of action, expected efficacy, common adverse effects (acneiform rash, diarrhea, paronychia, dry skin, stomatitis), less common but serious side effects (interstitial lung disease/pneumonitis, QT prolongation, cardiomyopathy — rare), and the importance of reporting new symptoms promptly
   - Will prescribe osimertinib 80 mg daily; patient to start at the next visit or upon receipt of specialty pharmacy approval
   - Prophylactic skincare regimen discussed (gentle soap-free cleanser, emollient moisturizer, broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen); patient to contact clinic for topical or oral antibiotic management if acneiform rash develops
   - Loperamide PRN prescribed for management of potential diarrhea
   - Glucose monitoring to continue given diabetes (PI3K pathway involvement by EGFR TKIs can occasionally affect glucose homeostasis marginally)

2. Radiation oncology referral:
   - Radiation oncology involved at today's tumor board; will formally see patient in separate consultation
   - Plan to reassess for potential consolidation thoracic radiotherapy after initial systemic response assessment at approximately 3 months
   - Radiosurgery or other brain-directed therapy not indicated at this time (brain MRI negative for metastases)

3. Restaging/response assessment:
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast planned at approximately 3 months after initiating osimertinib
   - Serial brain MRI surveillance to be incorporated into monitoring schedule given the known predilection for CNS involvement with EGFR-mutant NSCLC
   - Serial ECG monitoring at approximately 1 month and 3 months after starting therapy to monitor QTc

4. Hypertension:
   - Well-controlled on current regimen; continue lisinopril and amlodipine
   - Blood pressure within target range today

5. Type 2 diabetes mellitus:
   - HbA1c 7.2%, adequate control on metformin
   - Continue metformin 1000 mg twice daily; will monitor glucose more closely given oncologic treatment

6. Supportive care and ancillary services:
   - Referral to oncology social work for psychosocial support (new cancer diagnosis, significant life adjustment)
   - Referral to oncology dietitian for nutritional counseling and diabetes management in the setting of cancer treatment
   - Tobacco cessation already achieved (8 years); patient reinforced on importance of maintaining abstinence
   - Pulmonary rehabilitation referral considered given mild obstructive pattern on PFTs; deferred at this time given ECOG 1 and mild functional limitation, but available if symptoms progress
   - Advance care planning discussion initiated; patient and husband expressed wish to continue aggressive, curative-intent therapy; documents to be completed at follow-up

7. Clinical trial enrollment:
   - Patient made aware of potential eligibility for future clinical trials, particularly given the molecular profile (EGFR L858R, TP53 co-mutation, CDKN2A deletion). Contact information for the research team provided. No trial indicated at this time given first-line osimertinib is the standard and preferred approach.

Follow-up: Patient to return to thoracic oncology clinic in 4 weeks for on-therapy toxicity assessment and to review any initial concerns with osimertinib. Contact information provided for symptom management questions in the interim.

Time spent with patient: Approximately 65 minutes including history taking, examination, multidisciplinary tumor board discussion review, counseling, and documentation.

All questions answered to patient and husband's satisfaction. Patient verbalized understanding of diagnosis, planned treatment, and follow-up plan.
